title: Hatching eggs under a broody hen
subtitle: Using natural incubation for a small hatch.
excerpt: A broody hen will sit for 21 days, turn the eggs herself, maintain humidity from her own body heat, and cluck the chicks off the shells once they pip. She's the most reliable incubator you'll ever use for a small hatch. Here's how to set her up, what to check midway, and what hatch day looks like.
sourceNotes: Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive, broody management chapters. DEFRA/APHA poultry welfare codes. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg, poultry chapters.

glossaryTerms.definition: A hen whose hormones have triggered the instinct to sit on and hatch eggs; she will sit tight, fluff up, and reject being moved from the nest.
Shining a bright light through an egg in a darkened room to see the developing chick inside; used to identify infertile or dead eggs.
The first crack a chick makes in the eggshell, usually at the blunt end, marking the start of hatching.

projectSchedule.title: Set the eggs
projectSchedule.body: Place up to 12 fertile eggs under the broody at dusk. Mark them lightly with a pencil so you can identify any non-broody eggs added accidentally.

projectSchedule.title: First candle
projectSchedule.body: Candle the eggs in a darkened room. Fertile eggs show a dark mass and visible veining. Clear eggs are infertile; remove them.

projectSchedule.title: Lockdown
projectSchedule.body: Stop candling and handling the eggs. The chicks are positioning for hatch and turning now would disorient them.

projectSchedule.title: Hatch day
projectSchedule.body: The first pip should appear. Leave the hen undisturbed for the full day, hatching takes time and chicks need to dry under the hen before you intervene.

content.text: A
broody

hen is the simplest incubator available. She keeps the eggs warm from her body, turns them throughout the day, and manages humidity naturally. For a hatch of under a dozen eggs she's easier and more reliable than a cabinet incubator, and costs nothing to run.
Recognising a genuine broody

A properly broody hen sits on the nest from morning to evening, fluffs up and clucks when touched, may peck or growl when disturbed, and returns to the same nest each time you move her. A hen that sits for an hour in the morning and then goes about her day is not properly broody; don't set eggs under her. Test by moving her off the nest twice a day for three days, if she consistently returns within an hour each time, she's reliable.
Setting up the nest
Move the broody into a separate pen or hutch, away from the main flock. Other hens walking across her nest break eggs; roosters disturb the sit.
Provide a nest box at floor level, lined with soft straw or wood shavings. Place the box in a quiet corner away from direct sun.
Put three or four dummy eggs (wooden or ceramic) in the nest box and move the hen onto them at dusk. Leave her on the dummies for 24 hours before setting the fertile eggs.
Replace the dummies with the fertile eggs at dusk on the second day. Mark each egg with a soft pencil so you can identify them.
During the 21 days

Check on the hen once a day. Make sure she has food and water close to the nest, she will leave the eggs for 15,20 minutes each morning to eat, drink, and defecate. If she hasn't left in two days, lift her off gently and place her next to the water. She will return to the nest within minutes.
At day 7,
candle

the eggs in a darkened room. Fertile eggs show dark internal mass and fine red veining around a clear air cell at the blunt end. Infertile eggs are clear throughout. Remove clear eggs, they take up space and may crack and contaminate the others.
Hatch day
The first
pip

appears at around day 21, though the range for a naturally incubated hatch is day 20,23. Once a chick pips, it may take up to 24 more hours to work around the shell. Don't assist, intervening too early pulls chicks out before the yolk sac is absorbed.

After 24 hours from the first pip, check quietly under the hen. Dry, fluffy chicks clucking softly are fine. Wet, still chicks that have been out of the shell for an hour need warmth, the hen usually handles this herself, but in a cold pen you may need to move a non-responsive chick to a heat lamp.
After hatching

The hen will continue to sit for up to two days after the hatch starts, covering any slow eggs. Move unhatched eggs that are still intact (not cracked) at 48 hours post-hatch, they are either infertile or dead in shell. Remove them from under the hen and dispose of them rather than leaving them to contaminate the nest.
